nativeRange.habitat: Fox Coral is found in turbid, nutrient-rich reef environments, including lagoons, reef slopes, and protected reef zones characterized by lower light levels and gentle water movement.

decorPreferences: stable base with no toppling risk

diet.notes: Fox Coral is primarily photosynthetic, relying on its symbiotic zooxanthellae for a significant portion of its nutrition. It has been reported to benefit from weekly target feeding of micro-plankton, mysis shrimp, or brine shrimp, with polyps visibly extending during feeding events. Because no tentacles are present, feeding occurs through direct contact with the expanded fleshy tissue lobes.

goodWith: other peaceful LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with long sweeper tentacles
species that may nip at tissue lobes

imageCandidates.notes: Dan Schofield, 2048x1536. In-situ colony showing the diagnostic ruffled fleshy tissue lobes with green edges over a pink base and no sweeper tentacles; sharp, well-lit, whole colony framed.
Dan Schofield, 2048x1536. Sharp in-situ shot of a tan/pink colony with the characteristic radiating ridges and ruffled lobe margins; clear water, whole colony visible.
Pauline Walsh Jacobson, 960x720. Different individual and photographer, warm orange/peach morph showing the bubbly ruffled lobes; slightly lower resolution, added for colour variety.

summary: The Fox Coral (Nemenzophyllia turbida) is a generally peaceful LPS coral with distinctive ruffled tissue lobes and no sweeper tentacles. It prefers low flow, moderate light, and placement in the lower aquarium — care considerations that make it best suited to intermediate reef keepers.
careNotes:
The Fox Coral is the sole species in its genus and is also sold under the common names Jasmine Coral and Ridge Coral. It belongs to the Large Polyp Stony (LPS) category, meaning its fleshy polyps are large relative to its calcareous (calcium carbonate) skeleton — a group that generally responds well to target feeding and requires attentive placement. Some recent taxonomic sources reclassify the species from family Caryophylliidae into family Euphyllidae; the older scientific name Pleurogyra turbida appears in some trade listings, but Nemenzophyllia turbida is the currently accepted name.

Lighting: Fox Coral is generally advised to be kept under moderate light (approximately 75–150 PAR, where PAR is a measure of usable light energy for photosynthesis). It naturally inhabits turbid, lower-light reef environments and tends to do poorly under high-intensity lighting. When transitioning a specimen from a dim setting, acclimate it gradually rather than placing it directly under brighter fixtures.

Flow: Low flow is strongly recommended. Fox Coral lacks the sweeper tentacles found on many other LPS corals, and its ruffled tissue lobes are particularly delicate — strong or direct water movement can damage or shred the tissue. Gentle, indirect circulation that keeps the water oxygenated without buffeting the polyps is ideal.

Placement: Lower areas of the aquarium — bottom rockwork or the sandbed — are generally preferred. The calcareous skeleton is notably fragile compared to most other LPS corals, so securing placement to prevent toppling is important; even a minor fall can crack the skeleton and expose tissue to injury or infection.

Feeding: Fox Coral is primarily photosynthetic through its symbiotic zooxanthellae (algae that live within the coral tissue and provide energy through photosynthesis), but has been reported to benefit from weekly target feeding of meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, micro-plankton, or brine shrimp. The feeding response is visible as expansion of the tissue lobes. Because no sweeper tentacles are present, feeding occurs via direct contact with the expanded tissue surface.

Aggression and spacing: Fox Coral is widely regarded as one of the more peaceful LPS corals — it lacks tentacles and produces no sweeper extensions. Even so, adequate spacing from neighboring corals is advisable, since it can be stung by more aggressive neighbors and has no means of defending itself.

Skeleton fragility: The skeleton is unusually fragile relative to most LPS corals. Handle with particular care when transporting or repositioning, as even minor drops or impacts can fracture it. This fragility, alongside the low-flow requirement and target feeding, is the main reason the species is generally considered intermediate rather than beginner-level despite otherwise straightforward husbandry.

Water chemistry: Standard reef supplementation is required — calcium 420–440 ppm, magnesium 1260–1350 ppm, and alkalinity 8–12 dKH.
breedingNotes: Fragging is possible but uncommon compared to other LPS corals, as the fragile skeleton makes clean cuts difficult. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs but is not practiced in the aquarium hobby.
